speaker: interviewer
text: Herzberger, and the date is November 17th, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Magda, we left off on the last tape with you describing the story where you jumped out of the window to escape the SS guard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was hiding. And I can't understand up to today how I wasn't found. You see, there are some things that you cannot explain. At that point, I was really weak. And during the day, then, you know, I-- I kind of mixed with the other people. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought that I'd rather sleep next to the-- to the corpses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went back to the camp and slept. I mean, everywhere were corpses, you know? So I slept-- I ended up sleeping next to the corpses, and dragging and-- and digging. And it was the end of the third week. I couldn't stand up anymore. I was-- you know how you feel when you-- I looked like a skeleton. And gradually, you know, you become like a-- like a skeleton. And I looked at my-- I was horrified looking at my legs and my hands, you know? Every single-- everything was sticking out, all the bones. And I realized that I must prepare myself for that, because there's no more hope for me, maybe, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was on the ground, and I was surrounded by these horrible corpses, and the pyramid, you know, in front. I thought I'm already partly dead. And I prayed to God I should die in peace. I said, oh, God, if that's-- that's my last day of life, I ask you three wishes that I could die in peace. Number one, I want to hold, for the last time, something living. I want to embrace life. I want to be buried next to something that is living. And I want my spirit should escape somehow, and some-- something from-- of me should still be living on. And as I looked, you know, it was-- Bergen-Belsen was in a birch forest. And I see these birch tree. I love birch trees. And it was so very desolate during, you know, the early spring. But it was April 15th, and I looked at that tree. It was close to me, but it looked like it was miles away because I could not move anymore. And I could see the buds, and those buds sprouted out of-- to me, life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- I don't know. Sometimes, you know, you have an unusual power that's given to you. I can't explain. You know, we-- we are very, sometimes, very analytic and very reasonable. I don't think I can explain what force [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] descended up in me, what force encompassed me, that I was dragging, you know? We had long fingernails, because they didn't give us anything to cut them, and I was putting my fingernails, and I was dragging myself. Just-- it wasn't far for me to the street. And I embrace its trunk, you know? And I felt, oh, God has granted my wish. I-- am very emotional because I still feel how I felt.

speaker: interviewee
text: I embraced life for the last time. I looked up at the blue sky and asked why are we condemned to death we thought. Just because we are a Hebrew generation, that's why we are condemned to death and persecution. And then I thought, well-- you know, I always try to be a positive person. Maybe I would be buried next to that tree. And maybe I'd be a part of a root. What an honor. Still a form of life. I will be a part of a tree.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hope, well, maybe if this is granted, my spirit will set-- be set free. And I closed my eyes, and I was totally prepared for death. And I heard a big commotion in the camp. And I thought, jeez, I'm on the other side. I was afraid to open my eyes. What am I going to see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, finally, you know, I opened my eyes. I could see the guards disappearing from the towers, because they-- we were surrounded by machine guns. And I see the British troops on their tanks coming into the camps. I really thought I'm in a delirium. Frankly, I thought I am seeing things that don't exist. I couldn't believe that I was liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still didn't know, at that time, if I am going to be alive. They were throwing some cans from their trunk-- tanks for us. And they were in awe. They took photographs, you know? It was horrible. The ground of Bergen-Belsen was covered with 10,000 corpses. The stench and everything was horrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Typhus broke out. Thousands of people died of typhus after liberation. And I had a friend-- you know, I had a story from Auschwitz that I didn't tell you. But that was another miracle. I cannot understand that. I remember-- you remember that I-- I was saved many times.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in Auschwitz, you remember how I told you that they were like sardines there. And it was, at one point, I have seen many people committing suicide. And one night, I woke up. And I thought, can I take this? I mean, I just want to go next to the fence and just maybe court death, you know? And I got up, and I was standing by my fence. And I put the question, to be or not to be? You know, I love Hamlet. And suddenly, I feel a shoul-- a hand on my shoulder. And who it was, this schoolmate of mine. And said, Magda, aren't you ashamed of yourself? I followed you. What are you-- are you courting death? He used that word. Let's go back together, you know? He said, you have your parents. Do you want to disappoint them? You are the only child. And we went back. But the interesting part, who was that schoolmate. I had-- I was with her in the elementary school. There were two girls, sisters. But in the camp she was separated from her sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were very poor. And my mother was a very, [INAUDIBLE] very wonderful person with a big heart. I was very young. I was in the first grade. And my mother always put some-- some snack for me, and we got some milk at school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had my snack, you know, and I was just about bite in it. And I see two hungry eyes fixed on me-- the two girls. And I couldn't swallow the food, you know? And I gave it to them, because I said, well, I go home and I have all my-- you know, all my food. I came home. That went on for three weeks, and I was ravenous. I came home. I ate twice from everything. And my mother took me aside. She said, what did you do? Did you throw away your food? I said, no. I confessed to my mother. And my mother for four years was sending, every single day, food to these two girls. And, you know, she was the one who put the hand on my shoulder. She said, Magda-- and you know she survived? And my mother, when I-- we lived in Israel for nine years, and my mother was living in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we were all to United States. And she said, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this girl-- you know, my mother was at the shopping window, you know, and we knew this girl for many years. And suddenly she said, Mrs. Moses, Mrs. Moses. And she was the one. And she told my mother about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she-- I have some-- this is just a few incidents. I have [INAUDIBLE] the way I found my mother. You know, there are things in my life that I truly believe that you have to have hope and faith. And you have-- you can-- you should never give up. In the most adverse situation, you should say, somehow, I am going to make it, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to be a fighter, in other words. You have to be a fighter in adverse situations. You have to be a fighter in your normal life. And you have to fight for the things that are important for you. My life was important for me. And my father told me, when I was a little girl, you have to fight for the things and make sacrifices for the things that are important for you. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you liberated? What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated on April 15th, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in-- see, it took me three-- three months to be able to walk. And they were wonderful. They erected hospitals. They accommodated us in the SS quarters and they became our living quarters. It's a long story. It took me three months to wobble. And then we were disinfected. We-- we were quarantined because the typhus was raging. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then, after three months, I volunteered to work on the typhus hospital. Everyone thought I'm crazy. But I wanted to do something for-- for my fellow prisoners. I was grateful that I was liberated. I felt I have a responsibility. I heard many last wishes, many confessions. I will never forget them. Then, after-- see, the joint organization, the Red Cross, helped us to repatriate. So they repatriated us. So after six months, I left-- we left a group, which were from the Cluj, the Transylvania, and other people in Transylvania, that helped us to-- to go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, you know, I had relatives in Israel who were Zionists, who were there-- I sort of have a list of my relatives and who went there, you know. And it was a cousin of my mother, and I wrote her a letter. I said, I don't know what to do. Should I go there? Young people could come to the United States. They would help them. Should I go home?

speaker: interviewee
text: The letter came late. I left already the camps. I decided I'd go home. It was a good decision. And we went again-- very primitive accommodation. It took several days. The Red Cross gave us packages. That was our food, you know. And then very primitive accommodations, because we had to stay overnight. And we traveled through Germany. It was horrible, you know, after war. And it was terribly bombed. Hamburg, I mean, the cities were just [? bare roots. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, we came to Budapest, you know? And Budapest is, oh, maybe 120 miles from our city. I don't know the exact mileage. And we-- we were-- when we arrived in the afternoon, they said, there's no way you can go now. You have to wait until the morning in order to get transportation. So we, again, got this accommodations by the Red Cross. And we had to pick up a package a few streets from where we were located. And we went to the group of women, you know, to-- my-- my friends to pick up the package. And I never was in Budapest, although it was close by. I don't know the city. But on the way back, I said you know what? I want to go walk by myself. Now, I don't have a good sense of direction. I tell you, I can-- I can get lost easily. And they said, why do you to go in a strange city all by yourself? Because I want to think. What do I do if I go home and I find nobody? What am I going to do? And I was scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, I went and went. And suddenly, you know, I was in deep thoughts. I said, by God, I got lost. I don't know where I am. And I said, I'm going to ask questions. The first-- first person who comes my way, I'm going to ask for direction. And I see a gentleman coming from the opposite side. But as he came closer, it seemed to me, oh, this is a familiar face. I have seen him somewhere. And when he came real close, I realized he was our next-door neighbor. And we stopped, and he said, my god, you are alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said he went back to Cluj earlier, and he did-- his family was wiped out, and he moved to Budapest. But then the next sentence-- and I imagine how happy your mother must be that you come home. I said, did you see my mother? He said, sure. He said, I was in Cluj. We had a square, you know, in the middle  of the city. And I met your mother there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know where she lives. She gave the address of my mother. That's the way I found my mother. And I thought maybe-- you know, he's looked at me. He said, I'm OK. He said, nothing happened to me in the camps. This is the right address. And so I went, and my mother was living in the apartment house on the second floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell you, my knees were shaking. You had to ring a bell. And I thought, what if it isn't true? And I rang the bell. And then I see an opening-- a window opening on the second floor. And my mother said, who is it? And I recognized my mother. I said, it's your daughter. And she ran-- I mean, we were like in a frenzy, you know? She ran down and I [INAUDIBLE]. I couldn't believe I found my mother in a strange way. I came in. We had a real small apartment-- one room with a little kitchenette in the back. But my mother already had hand-- handmade curtains and handmade little table cloths.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had one bed that we shared, because that's what remained from our bedroom. And on a chair, it was prepared for me, clothing-- second-hand clothing. On a shelf, there were chocolates for me, just in case-- just in case I come home. I came in, and the great miracle, as I came in, I see my piano. Now, you see, I love music. I write music. And I couldn't believe that my piano wasn't taken away. I went down-- I-- I leaned down, and I kissed my piano. And I kissed every single old furniture piece.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother said, are you all right? You really behave strange. I never have seen you doing those things. I said, mother, these are old friends of mine. It was really touching, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and then, my mother was very supportive. I decided I'm going to fight now for my grades, for scholarships. So I get to medical school. And then at the university, I met my husband. He was in a graduate medical school. And then I got married in-- I came back in 1945. And in the fall of 1946, I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eugene Herzberger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your-- where did your mother go during the occupation? Where was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, see, my mother never wanted to talk about her experiences. And I did that for years. She said, you never talk about-- talk about my experiences, because I don't want. And it was her wish that I shouldn't, and I respect that. She never wanted to talk. And she said, you don't-- if somebody asked me to talk about it, I don't want it. And this I do for the respect of my mother, because it was-- she never wanted to talk. And she said, I don't give you the permission to talk. If I want to talk, I talk. If I want to-- I don't want to talk, don't talk for me. And see, my mother died, and I loved my mom. And she died last year, and I think I want to keep it her secret.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father died in the [INAUDIBLE], and I have-- people came back who have seen, you know, my uncle. They got the typhus. They worked, and then they were transferred. They got the typhus, and they-- and they died, both of them. So now, I can't wait for them. I never will see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were married in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was married in 1946. So it's going to be our 49th wedding anniversary this month-- November 21st. And you know, I was so happy. I was in medical school. And I was saying, thank god. I got a full scholarship. I worked hard. So did my husband. He was on full scholarship, because they were ruined too. And-- but you know what happened. You come back and gradually see that it is becoming a place where you were, or you come back now-- you don't get back anything what you had, because now everything is for the state. Not only you don't get back, you can't open your mouth. And it was the beginning of the totalitarian system. And it became worse. You know, what-- what they did to Romania was horrible. Ceausescu ruined-- I mean, the communists-- communists ruined the country, the people. It was a rich country. It became poor. The people became poor. They were slaves working for nothing. And not even could they open their mouth. And I had-- I have two cousins. I still have cousins in Kibbutz Maagan, you know. A second cousin who established Kibbutz Maagan. They were-- they were, in 1939, they were Zionists, their sister-- they went with their sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had two-- a cousin of mine and two cousins of my mother were in the Mossad. You know? With Pales-- Palestine was on the British mandate. That was before the Palestine was-- became an independent-- before the State of Israel was established, proclaimed. And they-- you know, Israel knew-- I mean, the Jewish people knew, if the English goes out from there, the British, there's going to be war with the Arabs. So they wanted to organize aliyahs, you know, immigrations. And those were illegal, you know. We went through illegal immigration. And two of my mother's cousin came. There are very strange things in my life. And I said, you want to stay here for your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't like it anymore, because it was-- you-- you were whispering. You didn't know who's lea-- who is going to say that you are an enemy of the state. And at 2:00 in the-- at night, you are going to be taken and nobody will know where you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: So do you-- you don't want to-- now we are robbed of our spiritual freedom. How much do we do we take? Still was antisemitist to a great extent. They were still didn't like the Jews, you know? And we said, well, why should we-- I mean-- so we went with these two ships, and

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were 16,000 Jewish people immigrating in December, 1947. And naturally, it was an illegal thing. And the British got word, and we were intercepted in the Mediterranean by two British warships. And they say it's going to be war. Either you go to the Island of Cyprus, and you agree.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or we are going to fight you, and you're not going to be able to get Israel ever. And our captain felt-- there were many children and people, you know? He felt that we should-- I mean, we-- we were forced. It was not voluntary. We were forced-- accompanied by the battleship to the Island of Cyprus.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- we were put on the sea shore. We lived in tents for a whole year. There were very few physicians. My husband was a physician, and they really needed physicians. And there were 8,000 in our camp. And I worked as a nurse, because due to my medical school, they-- I knew how to get injections. So at the beginning, I was inoculating people. There's a whole story. And we were-- were poor. The-- our food was very poor. We got half a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, that is oatmeal. I never can look oatmeal for years, because you didn't get-- you got, once a week, a little meat. And you didn't get hardly any vegetables. And I-- I became pregnant there.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- I took my mother along. I said, mother, you are not going to stay. You come with us. And my poor mother said, what did I come for to stay here for a year like this? But we were together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and so, at the end of 19-- in '49, at the beginning of 1948-- in '48, the Jewish state was proclaimed. So finally, they let us go on February, 19. And two weeks after we arrived to Israel, my son was born. And Israel was, you know, was a big economic depression. I mean, everything was on rations. I had to be, for several months in a-- in a-- in a-- in a place for immigrants, you know. We were big groups. It's a whole story. My Cyprus story is a big story. And they were some hard years. You know, Israel's strength are its people. They are fierce fighters, and they have to be, because otherwise, they would have been annihilated. I don't know. With Jewish people, we are-- we have to be fighters, for one thing or another. But you still want to have a Jewish state that you can call yours. And that's why we went. I said, finally, we go to somewhere, you know, where-- where we're not going to be persecuted. And my husband then specialized there under Dr. Ashkenazi as a neurosurgeon. He was one of the most famous neurosurgeons in Romania. He was Jewish. He came back. He said, I had enough. I don't want to stay. And he was trained in France. He was very, very talented. And then my husband got-- see, the neurosurgical training is five years. It's a long time. But you know, in Israel, there was lots of war, all the-- at the borders. They had so many people, you know, shot. And Israel was always-- I was in Israel for nine years. There was never peace. And then he had a neurosurgical department of his own for five years before we came-- at a large hospital. It was in near Tel Aviv, you know. And-- and-- but you see, in 1956, I-- I experienced the war. My husband was mobilized, you know-- the Sinai Campaign.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was with my two kids. We had an apartment on the second floor, and in the sirens were-- were, you know, and I was wondering, where am I going to go, you know, if something comes? My son was five years old, and my daughter was just a baby, you know? Your children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry and Monica. And they were just babies. And I remember, you know, I was very scared when I heard the sirens, because my experience of the war still were with me. And then my son, Henry, said, mother, I don't want to die. Will you let me die? I said, no, Henry, by no means. I'm never going to let you die. And then I-- I was so scared for myself. And I was carrying my baby, and he-- he's hanging on to my hand. There are-- you know, at one point, we decided to emigrate to the United States. You know that my husband, professionally, had a very beautiful positions. But there are lots of disturbing things. And if me, personally, I am disturbed by constant war, because you never know-- you know, in Israel, if it's in one day, the country can be mobilized. They are extremely organized. And it's a great country.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother stayed in Israel, because she married-- remarried there. And then she-- then after 10 years, she lost her second husband, 11 years. And then I was pleading with her to come stay with us. But my mother loved Israel. He has these cousins and friends. And she said, you want to make me happy? I come to visit you. And we were going at it. She was one year in Israel, two years at my place, then going back and forth. And do you know when I could convince-- I started convincing her in 1961. And you know when  I succeeded? In '89. [LAUGHS] She was a hard one to-- Let me go back just a little bit more to ask you a description of the camp in Cyprus. What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the camp in-- well, I tell you. I give you a description. We were-- we were stationed I think near from Augusta, if I'm remembering well. And-- but we were out, you know, on the sea shore-- our tents were right on the sea shore. And sometimes, you know, when it was stormy, I thought the sea was going to takes us completely in. We had-- we had to-- very primitive conditions. We had to wash ourselves and the laundry with salt water. When we left Cyprus, you know, we could tear up all our clothing. We were guarded by the British. And if you tried-- you know, we could see sometimes ships coming. If you tried to swim out, they would shoot you. You know, they could-- they-- they would-- you could not escape. Now, what happened, you know, while independence, the war was going on, they needed-- Like Dr. Ashkenazi was taken out from there. I don't know how they did it, you know. They needed some specialists. And I think that was the Jewish underground who was doing that. You know, things happen. Some physicians were taken out. But my husband was a very young physician, and they had-- they needed those in the camps. You know, I-- I learned to live with the sea and its moods. And I love the sea. I have seen its beauty and also its cruelty, in some ways. The first-- first time my husband erected a tent, it came a big storm, and the tent was getting up, and we got all wet. But you know, there were very many people. And when you have many people, our food was-- was-- was very poor. We had this half of-- I told you. But for instance, entertainment, there were people who were boxing. There were boxing matches, you know, and things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a hard life, and the hardest part was that we didn't know what's going to happen to us, you know? And-- and I was on night duty as  a nurse, and then if somebody came and needed a doctor, I went with the gas light, you know, in the camps. And I was worried that I might meet the snake. [LAUGHS] You know? And the mice-- the little field mice. We had some cookies, and they were stealing it from us, you know? It's-- it was-- you you know, we were exposed to the elements. But I think that if I wouldn't have worked, I would have gotten nuts there, not to do anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my poor mother was cooking. You know, we had two beds there. And then we had a little box that we transformed it for a cooking table, you know, a wooden box. And then we had a-- an oil something-- gas, oil lamp. My mother was-- very primitive.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a very, very primitive life. And after we came back, we came in very primitive, also, transportation. And we always-- we always had cargo ships, you know? We never had an elegant thing. But it was very hard. It was a hard year in Cyprus, especially being pregnant. And, you know, it was not easy.
